assignment: AT1 - Identify Opportunities for AI Task Automation

section1.title: Business Overview
section1.content:
"BlueBush" is an Eco-Friendly company that is dedicated to selling only products that tick the eco-friendly box. This covers are large range of bespoke homeware products. Premium sustainable Australian homewares. Ethically sourced, beautifully crafted, built to last.

They are an online only store, adding further sustainability to their business. Therefore, they are an online e-commerce business offering direct-to-consumer products.

The business is small in size only requiring minimal employed people to handle online orders, customer service, stock taking, and order deliveries.


Their Key Values:


  • Sustainability First
  • Community Focused
  • Quality Craftsmanship
  • Transparency

section2.title: Current Business Processes
section2.content:
BlueBush business processes:

  1. Review online orders. (Performed by Staff)
  2. Login to BlueBush Admin site.
  3. Review orders placed by customers.
  4. Confirm payment received.
  5. Confirm allocated stock for fulfilling customers' orders.
  6. If stock is missing, send email to customers providing options going forward.
  7. If stock is available, send order to dispatch team to prepare orders for delivery.
  8. Stocktake. (Performed by Staff)
  9. Review and record all current stock in the warehouse.
  10. Compare warehouse stock levels with online database of stock quantities.
  11. Make note of missing, lost, or stolen stock.
  12. Processing incoming stock and update online database stock levels. (Performed by Staff)
  13. Receive deliveries of stock.
  14. Categorise each stock based on product ID/variant.
  15. Update database with new stock counts.
  16. Create new product stock data for new products to add to the website.
  17. Source stock promotional photography
  18. Develop new product information to populate product webpage.
  19. Login to the Admin portal and add new product information to the database.
  20. Confirm the new product is live and stock levels are accurate.
  21. Preparing Customer Orders for delivery (Performed by Staff)
  22. Receive confirmed customer orders ready for dispatching
  23. Collect ordered items and and package for delivery.
  24. Organise package for delivery based on delivery method requested.
  25. Customer Support (Performed by Staff, facilitated by admin portal and email integration)
  26. Online Website Chat Support
  27. Dedicate person assigned as online chat support.
  28. customer support alerts system they are an available support person.
  29. customer support waits for customers to initiate a customer support request.
  30. When customer initiates customer support chat, the support worker is alerted and begins customer communications.
  31. Support person attempts to answer the customer question, searching their product information, manuals, etc for correct and relevant data.
  32. Direct Phone Call Support
  33. Dedicated support person receives phone call from customer
  34. Customer Support person attempts to answer the customer question, searching their product information, manuals, etc for correct and relevant data.
  35. Email Support
  36. The customer support email sends an alert to customer support that a new customer support inquiry has been made.
  37. A customer support person logs into the admin portal and accepts the customer support request, preventing other from accepting the request.
  38. Customer support reviews the customer's request and creates a response in the admin portal. The admin portal replies to the email and saving the communication trail to the database.

section3.title: AI Automation Opportunities — Identification
section3.content:
Potential AI Automation benefits:

  1. Review online orders.
  2. Review online orders.
  3. Correlate orders made with incoming payments
  4. Confirm allocation of stock for orders.
  5. Provide work orders for staff to gather customer stock, and package ready for delivery.
  6. Prepare shipping labels
  7. Automate delivery pickup requests for sending items
  8. Stocktake.
  9. Mobile device used to scan incoming stock.
  10. Accidental double counting eliminated due to each items unique package ID flags ignore if re-scanned.
  11. Compares previous stock levels and previous orders to maintain balance between incoming/outgoing stock.
  12. AI flags missing/stolen stock.
  13. AI determines potential time windows of missing stock items.
  14. AI logs all delivery data, company names, drivers name, time, date, destination addresses, etc.
  15. Processing incoming stock and update online database stock levels.
  16. AI scans incoming labels from delivery drivers when new stock is received. Confirms product items/quantities for worker to confirm order is correct or is missing items.
  17. AI confirms delivery is ready for storing in warehouse. Sends warehouse request and provides all the items in the delivery, and where in the warehouse each product should be located.
  18. Worker scans each item as they are logged into storage. AI then updates the online stock in the Supabase database.
  19. Preparing Customer Orders for delivery
  20. AI could read incoming orders, confirm that payment has been made, confirms warehouse allocated items are available, automate the process if stock is not available, create work orders for warehouse to fulfill online orders.
  21. Customer Support (Performed by Staff, facilitated by admin portal and email integration)
  22. AI could be used to inject all product information data from where it came from, how it was made, why it is eco-friendly, product specifications, stock levels, estimated delivery time, etc.
  23. AI would be training on LLM for natural language.
  24. AI could be used to manage email responses, social media and online chat communications, and even voice call communications.
  25. A fully trained LLM would speak like a real customer support person, have instant access to all dynamic product information, assist with customer website operations, like how to make orders, make payments, find products, or help with how to use products.
  26. This AI would free up workers time, allowing them the ability to work on other business operations that an AI cannot yet assist with.

section4.title: AI Automation Opportunities — Priority Assessment
section4.content:
Priority Ranking (1-5): 5 being High Priority:


  • Review online orders. Score: 3
  1. Impact: It provides some assistant with preventing issues before they become a problem
  2. Feasibility: It would be a fairly simple integration with systems that are current set up with database access.
  3. Risk: It won't take into account missing items in the warehouse.
  4. Urgency: It is not a huge benefit compared to other areas AI could be introduced.


  • Stocktake. Score: 3
  1. Impact: It would assist in performing accurate stock levels and make sure the online database is correctly representing stock levels for the online store.
  2. Feasibility: You would require training an AI on shipping labels and understanding how the label relates to expected delivery items, what should be included and how many items there should be.
  3. Risk: The LLM would need to be thoroughly tested, as if it has any issues, then the whole integration would be flawed.
  4. ﻿Urgency: Current stocktake processes are fairly refined. There is not a strong urgency for implementing this.


  • Processing incoming stock and update online database stock levels. Score: 3
  1. Impact: It would reduce errors in online orders being assigned to stock that is not available and allow for customers the ability to decide whether they wish to place backorders.
  2. Feasibility: Due to the requirement of providing a large quantity of an accurate dataset of of labels, which is possible, tailoring the dataset specifically for BlueBush may introduce unidentifiable outcomes, it would need to follow a supervised training method. This may add additional training time and hallucinations.
  3. Risk: implementing to early may introduce a nightmare of inaccurate stock levels until the model is at a high enough accuracy to never be wrong.
  4. ﻿Urgency: Due to this task requiring a substantial human involvement in either method, supervised training or manual stocktake, this method is best to train while performing manual stocktake. Therefore, the urgency is low and could be a progressive system implemented in a separate database. Once the the result of the training outcomes database matches or exceeds the human stocktake database, then this may be safely introduced.


  • Preparing Customer Orders for delivery Score: 1
  1. Impact: Due to the nature of the process of preparing orders. There is no real need to use an AI system to prepare orders when an automated system would perform with better reliability.
  2. Feasibility: N/A
  3. Risk: N/A
  4. ﻿Urgency: N/A


  • Customer Support (Performed by Staff, facilitated by admin portal and email integration) Score: 5
  1. Impact: The impact of reducing the requirement for staff to attend to general customer support requests is huge. The money savings, reducing the loss of time when staff are required to switch to a customer service role, then back to their original task.
  2. Feasibility: With current open-source chat models available, either Full fine-tuning or LORA fine-tuning a model with content based on the client's data will enable a natural speaking LLM AI Chat Bot that many will not be aware that they are speaking with an AI chatbot.
  3. Risk: If the trained model provides false data, there may be the potential of bad publicity for the website. This may not be serious and direct communication with a customer to resolve any issues they may be having.
  4. ﻿Urgency: The sooner this can be implemented, the greater the financial savings will be.

section5.title: Recommended AI Solution — Detail
section5.content:
Customer Support Chat Bot


Solution Implementation description:

  • RAG - For accessing dynamic data

  • LORA or Full fine-tuning:
  1. I've yet to determine what the best option for tuning an LLM from the base model to adjust it to the clients' requirements. Some elements to consider:
  2. If full fine-tuning, what implications could this have on the base model?
  3. Full fine-tuning might lead to risk of catastrophic forgetting - What could it forget and how much wasted training time/cost?
  4. Full fine-tuning will cost a lot more.
  5. Does the client want this?
  6. Does the benefit justify the cost for the client?
  7. How suitable/reliable will a simple faster LORA fine-tuned model be able to communicate with customers?


Specific functionality:

  • Speak naturally with a customer. unrecognisable to a human communicating in a support role.
  • Able to refer to the BlueBush database for up-to-date information, FAQ information, product specification, product manuals, stock levels, including pending orders not yet sent out from the warehouse.
  • It should be able to assist with product issues and provide solutions.
  • Troubleshoot issues with online ordering.
  • Provide additional information about products that may not be included in the online description.


Technology options:

  • ElevenLabs - They can handle voice synthesising and voice cloning, implement custom RAG data, they have native webhooks & tool calling.
  • Vapi - Low latency voice, allows for custom RAG, function calling for any API, and can integrate with external TTS systems.
  • Voiceflow - Seems like a very manual time-consuming process to setup.
  • Synthflow - Seems to be dedicated for Voice communications


Integration requirements: What existing BlueBush systems would it need to integrate with?

  • Currently the BlueBush website is set up on a "Supabase" database server.
  • This is a simple process for an AI system to access all the information RAG style to enable it to provide an AI system with the dynamic changes a website may have.
  • It will also include datasets prepared for LORA training or Full fine-tunning depending on the outcomes previously mentioned in "Solution Implementation"


Expected benefits: Quantify where possible (e.g., "reduce response time by X%", "save X hours per week")

  • Online Chat
  • 50% – 70% reduction in costs compared to real support staff.
  • Initial call response is reduced, as an AI is able to respond straight away. However, a response that is too fast will be a giveaway that the customer support person is an AI Bot. Including a time between request and response will greatly reduce the implications that the customer support is an AI Bot.
  • Many simple customer requests (40-60%) can be resolved without the requirement of a human being involved.
  • Improves Customer Satisfaction Score (SCAT) for quick issues; balances speed and cost; boosts sales conversions.
  • Email

  • Social Media

  • Telephone

task2.title: task2
task2.content:
Consider AI, ML, & DL Technologies:


  • AI (RAG)
  • The nature of BlueBush as a seller of homewares and the predictability of customer requests being related to products on the website, ordering processes, troubleshooting, and FAQs. RAG is the perfect option for a chatbot to retrieve and provide this information to a customer.
  • RAG Procedure:
  1. Convert FAQs, product details, documents, and website information into vector embeddings stored in a searchable database (Supabase)
  2. When a customer request is made, the LLM retrieves the relevant information and integrates it into a grounded response.
  • This results in an initial implementation that assists in responding with factual information, reduces the chance of hallucinations, which helps to prevent misleading information, and therefore maintains customer approval.


  • Machine Learning:
  • A supervised machine learning approach could be applied. This has the potential to reduce the current demand and incidentally reduce the customer response time by forwarding customer requests directly to those who can respond with the required information. A real-time triage instead of batching customer requests. This would achieve the following:
  • Removes the requirement for staff to categorise emails and forward them to their intended staff members.
  • Creates a real-time automatic triage, instantly forwarding customer requests to the correct location.
  • Direct impact on the customer response time and customer satisfaction.
  • Predictable and controllable based on defined fixed definitions.
  • Easy to test and validate
  • Low risk from unexpected outputs
  • It can be easy and quick to implement, without the requirement of a large infrastructure.
  • It has a strong foundation for automation.
  • However:
  • It is limited to predefined categories, causing it to struggle with vague communications, or the context could have multiple interpretations.
  • The initial setup of defining categories and labelled data can be costly.
  • New products and other updates would require retraining periodically.
  • The system is not innately a communication chatbot. It does not directly communicate with customers. If implemented as the sole solution. Customers only receive a standard response to any query they submit through the current request systems.
  • The method used to incorporate ML into the roadmap could improve the overall functionality and efficiencies by providing a more robust method for deterministic routing/classifications for either accurately redirecting requests or responding directly with customers. This will, however, require that I create a dataset with pre-defined categories for implementing Machine Learning into the pipeline.

  • Deep Learning/LLM Training - LoRA
  • Deep learning via LoRA fine-tuning does not provide data retrieval capabilities, but it enables the LLM to communicate in a style consistent with the BlueBush brand. It can tailor the communication style to alter the tone and style used to align with a realistic Australian use of communication.
  • LoRA Procedure:
  1. Source a solid starting LLM that allows for future scaling (PersonalPlex is personally under consideration)
  2. Source customer service datasets (Preferably Australian communications)
  3. Prepare a dataset that aligns with BlueBush customers
  4. Included other suitable Australian communication datasets to improve communication style.
  5. Fine-tune the LLM using LoRA to adapt tone and language style.
  6. Integrate the new LoRA-trained model into the existing functional RAG chatbot system.
  • This is an appropriate second stage in the roadmap to an AI customer service communication assistant. Providing all responses consistently while offering a customer experience as close as possible to a real human interaction, which complements RAG's implementation.

task1.title: task1
task1.content:
I am using the assignment example scenario for my assessment. I have fabricated BlueBush, an online store selling Eco-Friendly Homewares. The time needed to review, categorise, and respond has overwhelmed BlueBush's ability to respond to customer support requests, leading, in some cases, to a 48-hour response time. Currently, incoming requests are received by the client in bundles; this was an intentional option to reduce the overall time required to process customer requests. These requests are manually categorised according to the customer's request and then forwarded to the appropriate staff for a response. This process of reviewing, sorting, forwarding, and responding to a large volume of customer requests has worsened the response times to unacceptable levels.


The client wishes to investigate AI solutions to handle the majority of generalised requests made across email, social media, and website channels. My goal is to offer a solution that is iterative and scalable, growing as demand grows. It will need to improve response times, consistency of responses, and maintain a high level of customer satisfaction. This automation is aimed to reduce administration workload, allowing staff to focus on higher-value customer communications.


Their current customer communications system relies on, as mentioned previously, standard communication platforms: email, social media, and communications enquiries via their website.

task3.title: task3
task3.content:
The suitability of each technique to handle customer support queries across all channels, including email, social media, and the website, is a complex web that depends on the many requirements the AI is being used to solve.


Machine Learning can provide a solid foundation for the classification of requests, allowing for queries to be correctly distributed to the correct internal department to provide a prompt response. It also instantly removes a bottleneck in customer response time. However, training this into a base pre-trained model may hard-code information that would normally be accessed through using a RAG technique. This may introduce inefficiency, rigidity, and errors that would otherwise be avoided.


RAG will provide the Chatbot with the ability to access dynamic data from an online database of information. Relying primarily on a ML technique, each time a new product is added, there will be the requirement to re-train the LLM, whereas RAG will not. This makes RAG more suitable for environments where information is frequently updated.


ML would instead be used as a control layer, including intent classification (route queries), re-ranking retrieved documents, safety/moderation filters, and confidence scoring.


Similar to ML, DL should not be used to add dynamic data into the LLM's model. Fine-tuning should refine behaviour, not store knowledge. DL/Fine-tuning/LoRA should be used to improve the tone and formatting used in its responses.


Machine Learning (ML)

Advantages

  • Accurate intent classification and forwarding customer requests
  • Reduces response time
  • Used as a control layer (ranking, moderation, confidence scoring)
  • It can handle high volume

Disadvantages

  • It requires prepared, labelled training data
  • It's not great at understanding ambiguous text
  • Its ability to communicate naturally is limited.

Risks

  • Misclassification may forward queries to the wrong department
  • Learned Bias in training data can affect classification outcomes


Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

Advantages

  • Allows access to dynamic data
  • Responses are grounded in real data
  • New products are automatically included

Disadvantages

  • It is highly dependent on the information available in the database
  • It requires additional infrastructure (embeddings, vector search, indexing)
  • Retrieval errors can still lead to incorrect or incomplete responses

Risks

  • If information in the database is incorrect, it will provide incorrect information.
  • Security risks if sensitive data is exposed through retrieval layers
  • Latency issues if the retrieval systems are not optimised, or if there are database connection issues


Deep Learning (DL) / Fine-tuning (LoRA)

Advantages

  • Improves response tone, consistency, and formatting
  • Enhances domain-specific (BlueBush) understanding and language use
  • Allows alignment with brand (BlueBush) communication standards

Disadvantages

  • Does not effectively manage dynamic or frequently changing data
  • Requires careful dataset preparation to avoid degrading performance
  • Adds complexity to model maintenance and version control

Risks

  • Overfitting to training data may reduce generalisation
  • Amplification of biases present in fine-tuning datasets
  • Increased confidence in incorrect responses (more convincing hallucinations)

task4.title: task4
task4.content:
Proposal for Implementation of a Retrieval-Augmented AI System for Customer Support


This proposal outlines the implementation of an AI-assisted system to improve customer response times across the BlueBush website and associated social media platforms.


The objective is to reduce response bottlenecks, improve the accuracy of information provided to customers, and create a scalable system for future AI functionality.


Following a review of BlueBush’s current systems and customer interaction channels, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is identified as the most suitable primary AI technique.


The BlueBush website is already structured to source its content from a central Supabase database. This provides a strong foundation for RAG, allowing a language model to retrieve and implement real-time data when responding to customer requests.


This directly addresses key limitations identified in other AI techniques. It avoids hard-wired knowledge within the model, reduces the need for periodic retraining, and improves response accuracy by grounding outputs using live data sources. It is particularly suited to dynamic environments where product information, policies, and frequently asked questions are subject to changes on a daily basis.


Proposed Implementation Approach

The implementation will be delivered in stages to reduce risk and allow controlled testing:


Stage 1 – Data Preparation

  • Consolidate and review existing Supabase data sources
  • Clean and structure content for consistent retrieval (FAQs, product data, policies)


Stage 2 – Retrieval System Integration

  • Implement embedding-based search and vector indexing
  • Enable semantic retrieval of relevant data based on user queries


Stage 3 – Chatbot Integration

  • Connect the retrieval system to a pre-trained language model
  • Inject retrieved data into responses to ensure accuracy and relevance


Stage 4 – Supporting Systems

  • Introduce Machine Learning components for intent classification and query routing
  • Apply moderation and validation layers to manage response quality


Stage 5 – Testing and Deployment

  • Conduct internal testing using real-world queries
  • Monitor response accuracy, latency, and failure cases
  • Gradually deploy across the website and social platforms


Scalability and Future Expansion

This approach supports future expansion without requiring ground-up redesign. Once established, the system can be extended to include Machine Learning and Deep Learning enhancements such as improved intent handling, response optimisation, and conversational refinement.

It also provides a pathway for future integration of voice-based interaction, including full-duplex communication, aligning with longer-term development goals for more natural, human-like interaction.


Risks and Considerations

  • Data quality risk: Inaccurate or poorly structured data will directly impact response quality
  • Retrieval accuracy: Ineffective indexing may result in incomplete or irrelevant responses
  • Security: Access controls must be applied to prevent exposure of sensitive data
  • Performance: Retrieval latency must be managed to maintain acceptable response times


These risks can be reduced through data governance processes, structured testing, and staged deployment.


Submission and Consultation

As discussed with my lecturer. My assessment scenario, while grounded by the example provided in this assessment, diverges from the (course-aligned) image classification training outcome. While this may be the case, my lecturer was ok with me pursuing this and wished to express where complications may exist that may prove demanding in completing the assessments going forward.

task5.title: task5
task5.content:
Prepare a short presentation for giving to the class.


  • summarising your findings from Tasks 1–4
  • highlighting the key aspects of your recommendation 
  • rationale behind your chosen solution
  • address any questions they may have


You must complete the following observation checklist items:

  • Student incorporated relevant feedback into final presentation.
  • Student presented an opportunity to the class to apply AI, ML, & DL technologies in the workplace.
